sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Level 1 completed! The level_completed=True confirms that shifting the boundary left from state 9 completed the level. The new level 2 has a different layout.
Looking at the new board:
- Blue blocks on the left side (cols 0-3)
- Black horizontal bars with green (light green, N) segments
- A charcoal block with a green strip at the bottom
- Gray and white regions
The pattern seems similar to level 1 but with different colors and positions. Let me inspect the segmentation to understand the new layout.
From the segmentation:
- id=0, color=P, pixels=64, bbox=(0,0)-(0,63) - pink bar at top (HUD)
- id=1, color=W, pixels=972, bbox=(1,0)-(19,51) - white region
- id=2, color=G, pixels=2264, bbox=(1,8)-(63,63) - gray region
- id=3, color=b, pixels=16, bbox=(16,0)-(19,3) - blue block
- id=4, color=B, pixels=224, bbox=(20,0)-(23,55) - black bar
- id=5, color=b, pixels=16, bbox=(24,0)-(27,3) - blue block
- id=6, color=W, pixels=160, bbox=(24,0)-(39,11) - white region
- id=7, color=b, pixels=16, bbox=(36,0)-(39,3) - blue block
- id=8, color=B, pixels=112, bbox=(40,0)-(43,27) - black bar
- id=9, color=N, pixels=8, bbox=(40,28)-(43,29) - light green segment in black bar
- id=10, color=B, pixels=56, bbox=(40,30)-(43,43) - black bar
- id=11, color=b, pixels=16, bbox=(44,0)-(47,3) - blue block
- id=12, color=W, pixels=144, bbox=(44,0)-(63,7) - white region
- id=13, color=N, pixels=12, bbox=(52,8)-(57,9) - light green strip
- id=14, color=c, pixels=16, bbox=(52,10)-(57,13) - charcoal block
So the layout is:
- Blue blocks on the left (cols 0-3) at rows 16-19, 24-27, 36-39, 44-47
- Black bars at rows 20-23 and 40-43
- Light green segment in the lower black bar at cols 28-29
- Charcoal block at rows 52-57, cols 10-13
- Light green strip at rows 52-57, cols 8-9
The light green strip is at cols 8-9, and the light green segment in the black bar is at cols 28-29. The goal is likely to align them by shifting the boundary.
In level 1, I clicked the blue blocks on the right side. Here, the blue blocks are on the left side. Let me try clicking them to see what happens.
The blue blocks are at:
- Rows 16-19, cols 0-3 (upper blue)
- Rows 24-27, cols 0-3 (middle blue)
- Rows 36-39, cols 0-3 (lower blue)
- Rows 44-47, cols 0-3 (bottom blue)
In level 1, I clicked the upper blue block (row 25, col 61) to shift right and the lower blue block (row 33, col 61) to shift left. Here, the blue blocks are on the left, so maybe clicking them shifts the boundary in different directions.
Let me try clicking the upper blue block first to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Level 2 started. New layout with blue blocks on left side.
# Need to understand the mechanics and goal.

print(f"Level: {current_frame.level}, Step: {current_frame.step}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nNodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")

# Key objects:
# - Blue blocks at cols 0-3, rows 16-19, 24-27, 36-39, 44-47
# - Black bars at rows 20-23 and 40-43
# - Light green (N) segment at cols 28-29 in lower black bar
# - Charcoal block at rows 52-57, cols 10-13
# - Light green strip at rows 52-57, cols 8-9

# Try clicking upper blue block (row 17, col 1)
print("\nTrying click on upper blue block at (17, 1):")
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 17, 'col': 1}], intent='solve')
print(f"  gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"  level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Charcoal now at cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        break

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Green strip at cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        break

print(f"\nCurrent state: {state_graph.current}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
